But then I thought to myself, 'This is my dad's generation who went through hard times; they'd know if this were real.' Same goes for social media today.\n\nAlso, don't forget to be critical of what you see. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
Managing fake content on social media is a tricky business, especially for someone like me who spends just an hour each day scrolling through their feeds. I've seen plenty of misleading ads and outright scams trying to pop up, often disguised as harmless posts from friends or family. My advice would be to stick with the basics—verify sources whenever something catches your eye, don't click on suspicious links unless you're absolutely sure they're legit.

I remember when my cousin sent me that link for a miracle cure for hair loss, I almost fell for it because I was worried about grays creeping in. But then I thought to myself, 'This is my dad's generation who went through hard times; they'd know if this were real.' So I called him and he said nope, not buying into that nonsense.

Another thing is to report fake content when you see it. It might seem tedious, but every time helps clean up the platform a bit. Plus, with my dad's degree in business, he taught me about due diligence—double-checking things before acting on them. Same goes for social media today.

Also, don't forget to be critical of what you see. My kids play videogames and they're always complaining about fake reviews online; I told them that just like with hair products in the shop, if it looks too good to be true, it probably is. That saying comes to mind often when sifting through social media.

If something feels off—spelling errors galore or requests for personal info out of the blue—give it a miss. I used to get these emails about inheritance scams because my grandfather was from Europe and they thought he might still be using old bank details. Saved me some headache.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: I've spent one hour daily on social media, so I have firsthand experience with common fake content scenarios. Drawing from my father's business education (bachelor's degree) and personal experiences dealing with scams in family matters, I emphasize verification, critical thinking, and reporting as effective strategies. My low agreeableness helps me stay firm when rejecting dubious claims.
response_parsed.tone: Conversational yet cautionary; blending personal anecdotes with practical advice
